name: Jørundgaard (the valley farm)
certaintyNote: Kristin's childhood home, Jørundgaard in the parish of Sil, is drawn from the real Gudbrandsdal valley around Sel where Undset set it; sited there.
note: On her father's prosperous farm in the Gudbrandsdal valley, Kristin grows up loved and devout under the gentle, upright Lavrans. Undset draws the settled world of a medieval Norwegian estate, its seasons, faith and family honour, the ordered life against which Kristin's headstrong passion will break.

sources.ref: Kristin Lavransdatter, Wikipedia, on Kristin's home and Lavrans

name: The convent at Oslo
certaintyNote: Kristin is sent to a convent at Oslo, the real medieval town, where she meets Erlend; sited in the city.
note: Sent to the convent at Oslo to be schooled and to cool after a scandal, Kristin instead meets the charming, disreputable Erlend Nikulaussøn and falls hopelessly in love. In the medieval town she breaks her betrothal in all but name, setting herself against her father and her faith in a choice she will spend her life answering for.

sources.ref: Kristin Lavransdatter, Wikipedia, on Kristin and Erlend

name: Husaby (Erlend's estate)
certaintyNote: Erlend's estate of Husaby in Trøndelag, where the married Kristin runs the household and bears her sons, is drawn from the manor lands of medieval Trøndelag; sited in that region.
note: As mistress of Erlend's northern estate of Husaby, Kristin bears seven sons and holds the household together while her reckless husband stumbles into treason and ruin. Undset's second volume is a great study of marriage and motherhood, the passion of the first book hardening into the daily labour, quarrels and endurance of a shared life.

sources.ref: Kristin Lavransdatter, Wikipedia, on Husaby and the marriage

name: Nidaros (the cathedral)
certaintyNote: Kristin makes pilgrimage to the shrine of St Olav at Nidaros, the real cathedral city now called Trondheim; sited there.
note: Burdened by guilt, Kristin walks barefoot in pilgrimage to the great cathedral of Nidaros to lay her sin before the shrine of St Olav. The cathedral city, heart of medieval Norwegian faith, is where Undset gathers the trilogy's deep religious current, the lifelong struggle between Kristin's earthly passions and her longing for grace.

sources.ref: Kristin Lavransdatter, Wikipedia, on the pilgrimage to Nidaros

name: The convent and the plague
certaintyNote: Widowed and alone, Kristin ends her life as a lay sister at a convent in Trøndelag when the Black Death arrives in 1349; the convent is drawn from the religious houses of the region and sited there.
note: In old age, having lost Erlend and given her remaining wealth to a Trøndelag convent, Kristin becomes a lay sister and, when the Black Death comes in 1349, dies nursing its victims. Undset closes her vast trilogy on this act of self-forgetting love, the wilful girl of the wreath at last finding, in service and death, the peace that eluded her.

sources.ref: Kristin Lavransdatter, Wikipedia, on the ending and the plague
